rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF DANIEL M. POMMON JR.
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #9, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 07/03/2024, as the following issues (see tentative ruling for 04/24/2024 hearing) remain unaddressed: 1) No proposed order has been received. 2) No financial account statements verifying cash balances as of 09/20/2023 are on file. 3) The proof of service does not show notice was given to the attorney for the person who requested special notice. 4) The petitioner appears to have paid herself $1,440 as fees on account prior to the conservatee's death, in addition to $10,800 paid during the supplemental account period. The total unpaid balance must be further reduced by $1,440. 5) The ending balance of the supplemental account period stated on the summary of account does not match the ending balance stated on Schedules E & F. 6) A specific amount of net cash distributable to the personal representative of the conservatee's estate, after payment of fees and costs sought to be approved, must be stated in the petition and proposed order. 7) The court-appointed attorney for the conservatee has not petitioned for fees and costs, although her creditor claim was filed in the conservatee's probate estate case more than 2 years ago.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years
rulings.Rulings: (PART 1 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Respondent Brian Hee's Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Bring Case to Trial Within Five Years. The Motion seeks an order dismissing Petitioner Linda Hee's Petition to Compel a Full Account; Objections to Trustee's Account; To Compel a Full Report of Information Relating to the Administration of the Trust; To Return Property to the Trust and for Double Damages; To Compel Distribution of the Trust Estate Following the Death of Trustor Edward L. Hee; To Remove the Trustee for Breach of Trust and Appoint a Successor Trustee; For Surcharge Against the Trustee; and for Attorneys' Fees and Costs, filed on November 29, 2012 (the "Petition"), and any other subsequent petition or adversarial proceeding filed in connection with the Petition, or arising from or related thereto. For the reasons that follow, the Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Bring Case to Trial Within Five Years is GRANTED. Linda Hee ("Petitioner"), beneficiary of the Edward L. Hee and Bernadette W. Hee Living Trust dated March 12, 2002 (the "Edward Trust"), initiated this action by filing her Petition on November 29, 2012. Trustee Brian H. Hee ("Respondent") filed a Response/Answer on January 15, 2013. On May 6, 2013, Respondent filed his First Accounting Petition, which Petitioner opposed on September 6, 2013. Respondent filed a Second Accounting Petition on December 3, 2013, which Petitioner also opposed on February 19, 2014. On May 9, 2014, the court suspended Respondent as trustee and appointed Petitioner as temporary successor trustee of the Edward Trust. A trial date was set for June 22, 2015. Prior to trial, the parties attended mediation and reached a settlement. A Notice of Settlement was filed by Petitioner on June 17, 2015. On February 6, 2017, Petitioner advised the court for the first time of issues with the trust properties not disclosed in mediation, that in her opinion rendered the settlement agreement unenforceable. The Court urged the parties to return to mediation and set a further conference for status of settlement. During this time, litigation continued, with Petitioner petitioning for authority to sell trust real estate, which was granted. On August 31, 2018, Petitioner sought and obtained the permanent removal of Respondent as trustee, and her own appointment as permanent trustee of the Edward Trust. Almost four years after the settlement was reached, Petitioner sent Respondent a formal notice of rescission by mail on March 19, 2019. Three months later, Petitioner filed a Notice of Rescission of Settlement Agreement and Request to Restore Proceeding to Trial Calendar, on June 19, 2019. Respondent responded by filing a Petition to Approve and Confirm the Settlement Agreement. Also around this time, Co-settlor and beneficiary of the Edward Trust, Bernadette Hee (now deceased), appeared in the action by filing a Petition to Compel Former Trustee to Render a Forensic Accounting. On October 22, 2019, the Court granted an order allowing Petitioner to obtain a forensic accounting; Respondent's motion to confirm the settlement agreement was denied. Petitioner, as trustee, was authorized to engage the services of a forensic accountant to prepare an accounting of the Trust assets from March 12, 2002 through May 9, 2014. Respondent was ordered to cooperate in providing information or be subject to surcharges and sanctions should he fail to do so. (SEE PART 2 OF 6)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruiling).
rulings.Rulings: (PART 2 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) In the ensuing years since the court authorized Petitioner to obtain a forensic accounting and petition for its approval, Petitioner failed to do so in a timely manner. Tiring of the failure to move the case along, on November 11, 2022, the court took the Status of Forensic Accounting Petition off calendar. Almost a year later on September 5, 2023, Petitioner finally filed her Petition Presenting Forensic Accounting and Claims Against Former Trustee Brian Hee for (1) Breach of Trust and Surcharge of Trustee, (2) Return of Property; (3) Breach of Fiduciary Duty and Surcharge of Attorney-in-fact; (4) Financial Elder Abuse; (5) Finding of Financial Elder Abuse, and (6) Constructive Trust (the "2023 Petition"). On Janaury 29, 2024, Respondent filed the instant motion. Respondent moves the Court for an order dismissing this action pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 583.310 and 583.360. In the alternative, Respondent argues the Court has discretionary power to dismiss the action for failure to prosecute, under section 583.410. In opposition, Petitioner contends the 5-year period was tolled under section 583.340, subdivision (c) for (i) the period that Respondent fraudulently concealed material facts necessary to Petitioner's claims and (ii) during the period the settlement agreement was in effect. Petitioner further argues section 583.310 does not apply to the 2023 Petition because (i) it was brought by Petitioner as Trustee, (ii) it was filed by a different petitioner, and (iii) it alleges different facts and grounds for relief. Code of Civil Procedure section 583.310 requires that an action be brought to trial within five years. "The purpose of the statute is to prevent avoidable delay for too long a period." (Sierra Nevada Memorial-Miners Hospital v. Superior Court (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 464, 472.) Parties may extend the five-year rule by written stipulation, or by an oral agreement in open court. (C.C.P. SEC 583.330.) However, absent an agreement to extend the deadline, the action must be dismissed by the court on its own motion or on motion of the defendant if the action is not brought to trial within the time prescribed. (C.C.P. SEC 583.360(a).) This dismissal requirement is mandatory and "not subject to extension, excuse, or exception except as expressly provided by statute." (Id., subd. (b).) "Under the press of this statutory requirement, anyone pursuing an 'action' in the California courts has an affirmative obligation to do what is necessary to move the action forward to trial in timely fashion." (Tanguilig v. Neiman Marcus Group, Inc. (2018) 22 Cal.App.5th 313, 322.) "To avoid dismissal under the section 583.340, subdivision (c) exception, a plaintiff must prove (1) a circumstance establishing impossibility, impracticability, or futility, (2) a causal connection between the circumstance and the failure to move the case to trial within the five-year period, and (3) that she was reasonably diligent in prosecuting her case at all stages in the proceedings." (Tanguilig, supra, 22 Cal.App.5th at 323; see also Gaines v. Fidelity National Title Ins. Co. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1081, 1100 [plaintiff bears the burden of proving the "impossible, impracticable, or futile" exception].) (SEE PART 3 OF 6)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruiling).
rulings.Rulings: (PART 4 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) Petitioner appears to argue that the 5-year deadline was further tolled from "the time [Respondent] filed his accountings [in 2013] until the forensic accounting was prepared [in 2023] because [Respondent] fraudulently misrepresented and concealed material facts he was duty-bound to disclose." Petitioner's argument is as follows: Respondent filed false accountings in 2013 that misrepresented the trust's assets and withheld from the beneficiaries the information that would have revealed his misdeeds and the beneficiaries' entitlement under the trust. Due to Respondent's misrepresentations and concealment of essential records, a forensic accounting was necessary in order to support Petitioner's claims for breach of trust. During this time, the 5-year period was tolled because it was impossible, impractical, and futile to take the matter to trial without a reliable accounting. Petitioner's position is unworkable for several reasons. First, although Petitioner largely places blame for any delays on Respondent for allegedly not producing documents needed for the accounting, status reports filed by Petitioner attribute Petitioner's delay, at least in part, to having hired one forensic accountant who was not up to the job, and another whose other trial commitments prevented her from completing the job on time. Second, Petitioner does not cite any authority for the radical proposition that delays in discovery toll the 5-year statutory period. The forensic accounting is nothing more than Petitioner's assertion of what the true facts of Respondent's trust administration are, by way of supporting the claims against him that Petitioner initiated in 2012, and repeated when Petitioner objected to Respondent's accountings in 2013 and 2014. There is no case this court has found that holds that a plaintiff's failure to discover information pertinent to proving their case, extends the period for bringing the case to trial. (See, Bank of America v. Superior Court (1988) 200 Cal.App.3d 1000, 1016 ["Generally, delays encountered in discovery are part of the 'normal delays involved in prosecuting lawsuits' and do not excuse failure to bring a case to trial within the five-year limit."].) Respondent's alleged delay, even if true, does not constitute futility, impracticality, or impossibility that justifies any tolling of the 5-year period, let alone for 10 years as Petitioner seems to proposes. More importantly, it was neither impossible, impractical, nor futile to prosecute this case without a complete forensic accounting. Since at least the beginning of 2021, this Court has made it abundantly clear that the October 22, 2019 Order allowing a forensic accounting already ordered surcharges or sanctions against Respondent, should he fail to cooperate in providing the information. At a status conference on January 25, 2021, the minutes reflect that the Court advised Petitioner that, "[t]he time has come for the forensic Accountants to work with [what] they have and any gaps in the accounting will not go in [Respondent's] favor." Despite the court's directive, more than two and half years passed before Petitioner filed her Accounting Petition. (SEE PART 5 OF 6)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruiling).
rulings.Rulings: (PART 5 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) More importantly, Petitioner's counsel conceded that the lack of forensic accounting was not a bar to taking the case to trial. In an email to Respondent on July 27, 2021 discussing the 5-year statute, counsel informed Respondent that without a tolling stipulation, she would need to ask the court for a trial date and "take this matter to trial without the forensic accounting (but on the same evidence)." She goes on to explain that waiting for the forensic accounting "makes sense" because it would make trial "a much abbreviated procedure." A further email was sent in July 2022 wherein Petitioner's counsel asked Respondent if he wished "to continue to toll the period of time from the last hearing until the next hearing," which went unanswered from Respondent. Petitioner's argument that the 5-year period was tolled from 2013 to 2023 is not convincing, due in part because the evidence suggests that Petitioner actively sought a tolling agreement during 2021 and 2022 to pause the 5-year period. Finally, Petitioner contends that even if the 2012 Petition were subject to dismissal, section 583.310 does not apply to the "subsequent" and "independent" 2023 Petition that was filed in her capacity as trustee. The Court disagrees. Normally, "commencement" of an action is the date of filing of the initial complaint. (Tanguilig, supra, 22 Cal.App.5th at 322.) However, if an amended complaint alleges new causes of action that do not "relate back" to the original complaint, then the "commencement" of the action for purposes of section 583.310 is the filing date of the amended complaint. (Id. at 322-23; see Brumley v. FDCC California, Inc. (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 312, 320-325.) Thus, courts apply the "relation-back doctrine" within the context of section 583.310. "The relation-back doctrine requires that the amended complaint must (1) rest on the same general set of facts, (2) involve the same injury, and (3) refer to the same instrumentality, as the original one." (Norgart v. Upjohn Co. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 383, 408-409.) The gravamen of Petitioner's claims in the 2023 Petition are in substance the same as those made, or which could have been made, in 2012. In the original petition, Petitioner, in her capacity as beneficiary, alleged Respondent breached his duties as trustee during the preceding ten years. The petition sought an accounting, a surcharge against Respondent and requested the court hold him personally liable for her attorney fees. The 2023 Petition presents a forensic accounting done for Petitioner, as Trustee and, exactly as in 2012, seeks to hold Respondent personally liable for breaches of his fiduciary duty during the period from 2002 to 2012, and for attorney fees. That the 2023 Petition contains claims of financial elder abuse makes no difference; the claims against Respondent in 2023 are in substance those made in the original petition, even if some of the operative facts and theories of liability vary from those alleged in 2012. The Court finds the ultimate harm and general set of facts alleged by Petitioner are the same. The fact that now, because of intervening fact-finding, Petitioner can put together a better case, does not change this finding. (SEE PART 6 OF 6)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruiling).
rulings.Rulings: (PART 3 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) The initial Petition was filed on November 29, 2012. Respondent acknowledges that Petitioner was permitted additional time to bring the matter to trial under California Emergency Rule 10(a), which extended the time in which to bring a case to trial by six months for all civil actions filed on or before April 6, 2020. Respondent also concedes that the parties agreed to extend the five-year deadline in June 2021. Respondent's then-counsel Mr. Kass, and later Respondent himself, agreed in writing that the five-year statute would be tolled from June 28, 2021 "until the next hearing date," which was subsequently set for August 2, 2021 for status on the accounting petition. Emails between the parties demonstrates that Petitioner was aware of the approaching 5-year deadline stating in one email to Respondent, "there is a deadline for taking a matter to trial. Ours is going to run later this year." The August 2, 2021 hearing did not occur, and the status conference was continued several times without a hearing over the next few months due to Petitioner's failure to file the forensic accounting petition. The "next hearing" did not occur until May 11, 2022, when the court ordered the parties to appear. In addition, the Court finds the 5-year period was tolled for a 1,633-day period from May 4, 2015 (when the parties entered into the settlement agreement) through October 22, 2019, when the Court denied Respondent's motion to confirm the settlement. Respondent posits the settlement had absolutely no tolling effect because Petitioner took the position as early as 2017 that the settlement was unenforceable. The Court disagrees. A line of cases holds that "the time during which a settlement agreement is in effect tolls the five-year period, for the reason that attempting to bring an action to trial when all issues have been resolved through settlement would be futile" within the meaning of section 583.340(c). (Canal Street, Ltd. v. Sorich (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 602, 608; Schiro v. Curci (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 840, 844-845; see also Brown & Bryant, Inc. v. Hartford Accident & Indemnity Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 247, 254-255 [finding "futility" where trial issues have been resolved by settlement, even if the settlement later becomes null and void.].) Even if Petitioner had doubts as to the enforceability of the settlement, the Court still finds it appropriate to exclude the period during which it was in effect, giving Petitioner all benefit of the doubt. According to Petitioner, the validity of the settlement agreement only came into question once Petitioner began discovering that the values of the remaining trust properties were not as Respondent represented in his accountings. While Petitioner first raised concerns with the settlement agreement in February 2017, the record reflects that at least until February 2019, the parties were, to an extent, operating as if some sort of settlement were in place. For example, in November 2018, the parties were urged to engage in further settlement negotiations to resolve their issues and instructed to return in three months. At the next status conference on February 20, 2019, the trial court denied Petitioner's request to set the matter for trial based, at least in part, on Respondent's position that there was an enforceable settlement agreement. Respondent maintained his position up until the court denied his motion to confirm the settlement in late October 2019. Whether the Court views the settlement as a condition that made it futile to bring the action to trial or considers the settlement process to constitute impracticality, the period from May 4, 2015 through October 22, 2019 shall be excluded from the calculation of the 5-year period. (SEE PART 4 OF 6)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE THE EDWARD L. HEE AND BERNADETTE W. HEE LIVING TRUST DATED MARCH 12, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Bring Case To Trial Within Five Years (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruiling).
rulings.Rulings: (PART 6 OF 6 - HEE TRUST) Considering California Emergency Rule 10, the 318-day extension by agreement of the parties, and the 1,633-day tolling for the settlement agreement, the Court finds the 5-year deadline by which Petitioner was required to bring this action to trial expired on September 26, 2023. The "math" is as follows. The action was filed on November 29, 2012. Without any tolling, the 5-year statutory deadline would have expired on November 29, 2017. The six-months of tolling provided by Emergency Rule 10 extended Petitioner's time to bring the case to trial to May 28, 2018. This deadline was tolled for 1,633 days from May 4, 2015 to October 22, 2019 due to the settlement agreement. The 5-year period resumed, until June 28, 2021 when Respondent agreed to toll the deadline until the next hearing date, which the court finds to have been May 11, 2022. After tolling ceased on May 11, 2022, Petitioner had another 504 days to bring this case to trial before the time expired on September 26, 2023. She did not. Accordingly, Respondent's motion to dismiss must be granted. Respondent shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (END OF TENTATIVE RULING).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF PING WU
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. Au Ho, a devisee entitled to distribution of 7.272% of the residue, survived the decedent but died intestate in February 2022. Au Ho was a resident of Macau, China, at the time of his death. The estimated amount of net cash distributable to Au Ho is $28,769.76. No personal representative of Au Ho's estate has been appointed (or if appointed in Macau, no authenticated copy of letters issued to the representative has been filed with an original apostille), nor has each successor of Au Ho filed a small estate affidavit with an authenticated copy of the death certificate issued in Macau, as required by Probate Code sections 11801 & 11802. The Court is to determine whether this petition should be treated as a petition for preliminary distribution under section 11600 (not subject to the 50% distribution limit under section 11623(a)(2)), and if so, whether the statutory fees of the executors and attorneys should be allowed on account under section 10830 & CRC 7.701 (see also LRSF 14.55(A) generally allowing only up to 75% of statutory fees on account before final distribution), and whether one of the requirements of section 10832 is satisfied to allow the attorneys' extraordinary fees in part or in full. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF GLADYS G. GOSZULAK
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Direct Executrix To Sell Real Property, Or Alternatively To Remove Executrix
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 6/10/24 to be heard with the new report of status of administration and also for the following reasons. (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received by the assigned examiner. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) Dolores Tollestrup is reportedly one of the petitioners, but she did not sign the petition. [CRC 7.103(a).] if she is, in fact, a petitioner, her joinder to the petition must be filed. (3) Notice was not given to Dolores Tollestrup either. If she is not a petitioner, the hearing will either need to be continued for notice to be given to her or her waiver of notice must be filed. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (The examiner notes that item 5b of the I&A filed on 5/8/23 was checked, whereby Executor swore that she had filed the PCOR with the county recorder or assessor. Item 4 of the I&A was also checked, although a probate referee WAS required and appointed on 3/21/22.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF PERCY ORLANDO GREEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION, Appointment Under IAEA
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. Michael Storz, the first petitioner and professional fiduciary currently serving as special administrator, is a nominee of Lamonte Foggy, one of the children of the decedent's predeceased spouse. Joan Foggy, a competing petitioner, is a nominee of Darrell Brown, the decedent's brother. Joan Foggy has higher priority than Michael Storz under Probate Code section 8465(b). No objection has been filed by Michael Storz to Joan Foggy's competing petition to challenge her fitness to serve as successor administrator, despite her higher statutory priority. Note: The following issues have not been addressed by Joan Foggy re her petition filed on 05/07/2024: 1) Item 3d(6)(7): The total amount is incorrect. The bond amount must be rounded up to the nearest thousand dollars ($1,661,000). See LRSF 14.16(L). 2) Item 3g(2)(c) must be completed. 3) Item 3g(4) must be checked. 4) Item 5a(3) or (4) must be checked. 5) Item 5a(7) or (8) must be checked. 6) Item 5b: At least one box must be checked. 7) Item 6: At least one box must be checked. 8) Item 7b(a) must be checked. 9) Item 9 must be completed. 10) Attachment 8: i) The address of Darrell Brown is incorrect. Notice must be given to the correct address. ii) The address of Lamonte Foggy is incorrect. Notice must be given to the correct address. iii) The predeceased spouse's date of death is incorrect. iv) The deceased administrator's personal representative must be identified and receive notice. If there is no personal representative, all successors of the deceased administrator must be identified and receive notice. 9) Notice must be given to the competing petitioner and his attorney. 10) Notice must be given to each person whose request for special notice is on file. Copies of the petition and supplemental papers must also be served. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE PICHOTTO PROPERTY TRUST DATED DECEMBER 24,1993
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Accounting, Demand For Distribution, For Breach Of Trust, Return Of Trust Property, Financial Elder Abuse; Removal And Appointment Of Independednt Trustee; Removal And Appointment Of Independent Trustee, Imposition Of A Constructive Trust
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 07/17/2024 per counsel's request (via e-mail on 05/24/2024): 1) No proposed order has been received or lodged. 2) No DE-120 form is on file. A copy of the remote appearance information required by LRSF 14.1(C)(6) must be attached to and served with the form at least 30 days before the hearing. 3) Please provide a copy of letters issued to John Begg as personal representative of the estate of Shawn Crane. If no personal representative has been appointed, the name and address of each heir and devisee must be provided by a verified allegation, and notice must be given to each of them. See Probate Code section 17203(c)(1). Other issues may include: A) No summons has been issued and served as to the elder abuse cause of action. B) No declaration required by CCP section 377.32 has been filed by the petitioner to whom the right to prosecute Leona May's elder abuse cause of action is alleged to have passed under Welfare & Institutions Code section 15657.3(d). C) No DE-115 form is on file as to relief under section 850 against Shawn Crane's estate. If there is no personal representative, each devisee must be served in the manner required by section 851(a)(2).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DIEGO CARDOSO DE OLIVEIRA
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/10/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Items 5a(2) and 7a of the petition are contradictory. (2) Item 7(b) must be completed if the wife predeceased by not surviving Decedent by more than 120 hours. (Her parents or issue of her parents or next of kin, as the case may be, must be listed in item 8 and must receive notice.) (3) Notice must have ATTACHED TO IT a printout of the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (Notice of remote appearance is NOT required in probate matters and is not, in any event, notice to the interested parties of how to appear remotely at a hearing. Deadline is 6/28/24 for providing the assigned examiner courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.
